quality.rationale: 基于渐近平坦时空的ADM质量与角动量定义进行逐级展开匹配，逻辑链条完整且具典型天体物理背景

origin.question:
[文件 1: 1_s17_17.1.md]

Problem 17.1. Show that the constant M which occurs in the Kerr metric is the mass of the system, and that the constant a is the angular momentum per mass.

## Solution

Solution 17.1. One finds the mass and angular momentum of an asymptotically Minkowskian (flat) metric by finding a coordinate system in which it takes the form
$$
\mathrm {g}_{0 0} = - \left(1 - \frac {2 \tilde {\mathrm {M}}}{\mathrm {r}} + \mathcal {O} (\mathrm {r}^{- 2})\right) \tag {1}
$$
$$
\mathbf {g}_{0 j} = - \left(4 \varepsilon_{j k l} \tilde {\mathbf {S}} \frac {\mathbf {k} \mathbf {x}^{1}}{\mathbf {r}^{3}} + \mathcal {O} (\mathbf {r}^{- 3})\right). \tag {2}
$$
The constants $	ilde{\mathbf{M}}$ and $	ilde{\mathbf{S}}^k$ which occur in the expansion are then the mass and intrinsic angular momentum (see Weinberg, Section 9.4 or MTW, Section 19.3).
Expanding the Kerr metric in Boyer-Lindquist coordinates in powers of $\mathbf{r}^{-1}$ gives the leading terms
$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathrm {d s}^{2} = - \left(1 - \frac {2 \mathrm {M}}{\mathrm {r}} + \dot s\right) \mathrm {d t}^{2} - \left(\frac {4 \mathrm {a M}}{\mathrm {r}} \sin^{2} \theta + \dot s\right) \mathrm {d t} \mathrm {d} \phi \\\ + (1 + \dot s) [ d r^{2} + r^{2} (d \theta^{2} + \sin^{2} \theta d \phi^{2}) ] \\\ \end{array}
$$
Transforming to cartesian coordinates by $\mathbf{x} \equiv \mathbf{r} \sin \theta \cos \phi$ , $\mathbf{y} \equiv \mathbf{r} \sin \theta \sin \phi$ , $\mathbf{z} \equiv \mathbf{r} \cos \theta$ gives
$$
\begin{array}{l} d s^{2} = - \left(1 - \frac {2 M}{r} + \dot s\right) d t^{2} - \left(\frac {4 a M}{r^{3}} + \dot s\right) (x d y - y d x) d t \tag {3} \\\ + (1 + \dot s) (\mathrm {d} x^{2} + \mathrm {d} y^{2} + \mathrm {d} z^{2}) \\\ \end{array}
$$
and comparison with Equations (1) and (2) gives at once
$$
\tilde {\mathbf {M}} = \mathbf {M}
$$
$$
\tilde {\mathbf {S}} = \mathbf {a M} \mathbf {e}_{\hat {\mathbf {z}}}^{-}.
$$
origin.answer: Mass is identified as $M$, and angular momentum per unit mass is identified as $a$.
origin.solution:
One finds the mass and angular momentum of an asymptotically Minkowskian (flat) metric by finding a coordinate system in which it takes the form
$$
\mathrm {g}_{0 0} = - \left(1 - \frac {2 \tilde {\mathrm {M}}}{\mathrm {r}} + \mathcal {O} (\mathrm {r}^{- 2})\right) \tag {1}
$$
$$
\mathbf {g}_{0 j} = - \left(4 \varepsilon_{j k l} \tilde {\mathbf {S}} \frac {\mathbf {k} \mathbf {x}^{1}}{\mathbf {r}^{3}} + \mathcal {O} (\mathbf {r}^{- 3})\right). \tag {2}
$$
The constants $	ilde{\mathbf{M}}$ and $	ilde{\mathbf{S}}^k$ which occur in the expansion are then the mass and intrinsic angular momentum (see Weinberg, Section 9.4 or MTW, Section 19.3).
Expanding the Kerr metric in Boyer-Lindquist coordinates in powers of $\mathbf{r}^{-1}$ gives the leading terms
$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathrm {d s}^{2} = - \left(1 - \frac {2 \mathrm {M}}{\mathrm {r}} + \dot s\right) \mathrm {d t}^{2} - \left(\frac {4 \mathrm {a M}}{\mathrm {r}} \sin^{2} \theta + \dot s\right) \mathrm {d t} \mathrm {d} \phi \\\ + (1 + \dot s) [ d r^{2} + r^{2} (d \theta^{2} + \sin^{2} \theta d \phi^{2}) ] \\\ \end{array}
$$
Transforming to cartesian coordinates by $\mathbf{x} \equiv \mathbf{r} \sin \theta \cos \phi$ , $\mathbf{y} \equiv \mathbf{r} \sin \theta \sin \phi$ , $\mathbf{z} \equiv \mathbf{r} \cos \theta$ gives
$$
\begin{array}{l} d s^{2} = - \left(1 - \frac {2 M}{r} + \dot s\right) d t^{2} - \left(\frac {4 a M}{r^{3}} + \dot s\right) (x d y - y d x) d t \tag {3} \\\ + (1 + \dot s) (\mathrm {d} x^{2} + \mathrm {d} y^{2} + \mathrm {d} z^{2}) \\\ \end{array}
$$
and comparison with Equations (1) and (2) gives at once
$$
\tilde {\mathbf {M}} = \mathbf {M}
$$
$$
\tilde {\mathbf {S}} = \mathbf {a M} \mathbf {e}_{\hat {\mathbf {z}}}^{-}.
$$
